name: Exit Guard Intent
summary: UX vocabulary for confirming before a region the user is in is torn down while it holds unsaved or in-flight work. The exit-guard member of the Guard protocol: arms on the cancelable dismissal of any region (document, route, overlay); the deny-outcome is a user-mediated confirm (stay / discard / save). The loss-of-state predicate is supplied by the guard provider, never by the intent.

scope.description: Which region's teardown is guarded — scale-invariant. document is the outermost region (the whole page leaving); route is a same-document navigation; overlay is an in-app dismissal (modal, drawer, panel).

condition.description: What the user is warned they would lose — the user-facing flavour of the loss-of-state predicate. unsaved = uncommitted edits (dirty form, editor); in-flight = work still running (an upload, a background task).

resolution.description: The user-mediated outcomes the confirm offers. stay cancels the dismissal and keeps the region; discard proceeds and abandons the work; save commits the work first, then proceeds.

presentation.description: How the confirm is shown. native = the browser's own generic, non-customizable prompt (the only option at document scope, via beforeunload); custom = an in-app dialog with bespoke copy and a save affordance (available for route and overlay scopes).

description:
Exit Guard Contract

When the user makes an intentful, cancelable attempt to leave or dismiss a region — closing the tab, navigating to a new route, dismissing a modal — and that region holds outstanding work, a confirm is presented before the teardown completes. The user, not the app, decides whether to proceed. Whether work is outstanding is decided by the guard provider; this intent only declares that a confirm is presented when it is, never how the condition is computed.

Per-scope vocabulary

  • document: the whole page is closing or reloading. The browser shows its own generic prompt (beforeunload) — the app cannot customize the text or offer a Save button. presentation is forced to native here.
  • route: a same-document navigation away from the current view. The app intercepts the navigation and can show a custom dialog with bespoke copy.
  • overlay: an in-app dismissal of a modal, drawer, or panel. The cancelable close action is intercepted; a custom dialog is shown.

Resolution

  • stay: cancel the dismissal; the region and its work remain.
  • discard: proceed; the outstanding work is abandoned.
  • save: commit the work, then proceed (custom presentations only — the native prompt cannot offer this).

What this intent does not cover

Only cancelable dismissal triggers are guardable — a close button, a route change, beforeunload. Imperative teardown that bypasses a guardable trigger (an ancestor calling el.remove(), a framework unmount) happens too late to confirm and is out of scope. The predicate, its provider, and the trust/enforcement boundary live in the Guard protocol, not here.

Composition

  • Built on the Guard protocol — the sibling of the entry-guard / access member (access control); both delegate their predicate to a guard provider, but the exit guard's deny-outcome is a confirm rather than hide / redirect / forbid / cloak.
  • The Form block and any unsaved-editor surface reference this intent rather than re-implementing a dirty-check prompt.
  • The Background Task Intent's navigation-guard dimension collapses to referencing this intent for the in-flight case.

uxResearch.title: Exit-guard prior art across frameworks and platforms
uxResearch.source: Web Everything navigation-guard / Guard protocol research (#129, 2026-06-10)
findings: Every major router exposes a leave-guard: Angular CanDeactivate, Vue beforeRouteLeave, React Router useBlocker, SvelteKit beforeNavigate — all gate a transition on a predicate and let the user cancel.
The browser baseline is beforeunload, which arms the document scope but can only render a generic, non-customizable prompt — no custom copy and no Save affordance.
Native platforms confirm the same pattern at overlay scope: iOS presentationControllerShouldDismiss / isModalInPresentation guard an interactive dismissal against a dirty state.

The unifying abstraction is guarding a region's teardown against a loss-of-state predicate, not 'navigation' — which is why this is the exit-guard member of the surface-agnostic Guard protocol, not a router-only feature.
uxResearch.keyInsight: Routing leave-guards, beforeunload, and native modal-dismissal guards are one intent at three scopes; the only real UX divergence is that the document scope is stuck with the browser's native prompt.

researchGaps: Reconcile a custom route-scope confirm with the browser's native beforeunload prompt when a single user action triggers both (e.g. a hard reload mid-route) — the user must not see two stacked prompts.
Whether 'save' belongs in this intent's resolution vocabulary or composes from a separate Save/Action intent, given the native scope structurally cannot offer it.
How the confirm copy is sourced per condition (unsaved vs in-flight) without the intent reaching into the provider's predicate internals.
